teachSlides.title: What is a stock sector?
teachSlides.body:
A label that groups similar businesses — technology, health care, finance, energy, and so on.

**Example:** Mall map: “food court,” “fashion,” “electronics.” Sectors sort companies by what they mainly do.

teachSlides.title: What is volatility?
teachSlides.body:
How wildly prices bounce up and down. High volatility means bigger mood swings on the chart.

**Example:** A calm bus ride vs. a jet coaster — both get you somewhere, but one jolts you more.

teachSlides.title: What is the P/E ratio, in kid-friendly terms?
teachSlides.body:
Price divided by yearly earnings per share — a rough “how many years of profit am I paying for?” shortcut. It is not perfect, but it helps compare similar companies.

**Example:** If a lemonade stand earns $10 a year and someone pays $100 for it, that is like a P/E of 10 — very rough math.

teachSlides.title: Why might a “hot stock list” still be a bad match for you?
teachSlides.body:
Because your goals, timeline, and nerves matter. A stock can be exciting and still too risky for your situation.

**Example:** A race car is awesome — not everyone should daily-drive it to school.

teachSlides.title: What is dollar-cost averaging (simple idea)?
teachSlides.body:
Investing a fixed amount on a schedule so you sometimes buy high and sometimes low — smoothing out the urge to time the market perfectly.

**Example:** Buying one pizza slice every Friday: some weeks it is on sale, some weeks full price — average evens out.

teachSlides.title: What is a dividend (basic definition)?
teachSlides.body:
Cash some companies pay shareholders from profits, usually on a schedule. Not all companies pay dividends.

**Example:** A club shares some snack money with members when the year goes well.

teachSlides.title: Why is comparing yourself to influencers dangerous in investing?
teachSlides.body:
You rarely see their full picture — losses, debt, or sponsorships. Your race is your timeline and budget.

**Example:** Comparing your first 5K run to someone’s edited highlight reel.

teachSlides.title: What does “market cap” mean?
teachSlides.body:
Roughly: share price × shares outstanding. It estimates the total value the market puts on the whole company.

**Example:** If 1 million shares exist at $20 each, market cap is about $20 million — a yardstick for size, not guaranteed truth.

teachSlides.title: Why read both bull and bear arguments before buying?
teachSlides.body:
So you understand what could go wrong, not only the hype story. Good decisions weigh upside and downside.

**Example:** Buying a phone: read 5-star and 1-star reviews, not only the ad.

recapSlide.title: ⚡ Level 3 — Intermediate
recapSlide.body:
Sectors, volatility, picking themes, and thinking about what “expensive” means.

Start with the first card anytime you need a reset.
